speaker: interviewer
text: Greenman in London, England. And this is tape number six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now where was I again?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the fact that you decided not to eat because you'd had the dream, and that you changed your mind.

words.text: and that you changed your mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I came to again. I start eating. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I gave my piece of bread to one of the boys walking around in the ward, so-called looking out for the sick in bed. And I wanted water. We were not allowed to drink water. I think that's the only time that I gave up my bread for a cup of water. I had fever, and I wanted water. Anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where are we now? About February, March of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We're still in January in Buchenwald. I arrive in Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, we're in the barrack, and we're waiting for the doctors, and the doctors send us, then, to the various barracks. And I was sent to-- with the rotten feet. So I'm laying there. And a lot of boys had rotten feet. And one morning, the doctor lifted up the blankets from every bed, and he'd look at the feet. And he said to the assistant, schneiden. Schneiden. That meant cut, cut, cut, operate. And when he came to my bed, he says, schneiden. So I says, [SPEAKING GERMAN] That meant-- what, I said-- please don't cut. These are chilblains. With the warm weather, they'll be all right. And I'm an Englishman, and soon the Americans will come here and liberate us. I don't know where I got the words from. A doctor never hit you. And he didn't cut my feet. I got my feet, less one big toe on my left foot that had to come off in France, when they sent me to France into hospital. So I've got nine and a half toes. And every time, they ache. If you ever look at them now, they're always thick and swollen and hurt with the bad weather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So days go by. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I see the doctor in my ward. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But before, before that, yes, I'm laying in still in the ward with the rotten feet, and they tell me, tomorrow morning you're going out. Your, your temperature's gone. Tomorrow morning, you're going out. [INAUDIBLE] Oh, gosh, now I've got to go out in the cold-- snow, cold. And that same-- same afternoon, and in the evening, I get a pain in my ear. And I'm laying there in bed, and there's one man-- non-Jew-- who looks after the sick. And every evening, they take up your temperature with thermomet-- thermometer. And there is another thing that somewhat happened to me in the other-- in the other sick barrack. And he comes to me, and he looks, and he says, you've got a temperature. What's wrong? So I says, [GERMAN]. I've got pains in the ear. So he looks at it, he says, [GERMAN]. And I had erysipelas, erysipelas-- contagious. So the next morning, the doctor put a wooden-- a crepe paper on my foot, and boy, that was ugly, dirty. And on a little barrow, I was taken to another barrack. And as I get into the barrack, I sits down on a wooden bench. And the doctor there-- I think it was a Russian doctor-- and he looks at my feet. The bandage was gone already. And he, he looks at me and looks at my ear. And then I must have said a few English words because he calls out to another doctor there, and the other doctor is Albert Kongs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Albert Kongs, he was on the resistance in Belgium, if he can hear me. And he came over, and, and this Russian doctor says, a Tommy, a Tommy, a Tommy-- an Englishman. So Albert Kongs talks to me in English. And I tell them who I am. And he says, right, you stay here with me. I'll look after you till the end of the war. And he says, find a place in the bed. And I climbs up into the bed after I put another bandage around my foot. And in those beds were men with-- blind, they went blind. Sick ears, sick cheeks, swollen up. All the same disease-- erys-- erysipelas. I got into my bed, and there was a sheet all dirty, dirty. Never mind. Well I stayed in that barrack, and while I could still walk about, I fed the people who were blind. There was one Belgian man who would talk Dutch like I could. We could converse. I help him with a spoon of soup in his mouth, to get it. He presented me with a pair of blue leather gloves, which they pinched off of me later on. Yeah. And I got his address, but I wrote him after liberation, but I didn't get no answer. I don't know whether he came out alive. Then there was Rotschild, Oscar Rotschild. This young fellow was-- escaped from Germany before the war, beginning of the war, or before the war, got to Groningen in Holland, where he had a man's clothing shop. He was arrested in Holland and sent to various camps. And he laid in his bunk next to me in Buchenwald. And he suffered very much with dysentery. He was no more than skin and bones. He was a nice fellow. And one morning-- I used-- I used to take him to the lav to clean him up. And one morning he said to me, take my bread and change it for a piece of ontbijtkoek. It's gingerbread, which the French prisoners received from home via the Red Cross. I said, if you eat that, Oscar, you'll have terrible-- more dysentery. He said, I don't care. As long as I get strong enough to get back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I changed his bit of bread with a piece of gingerbread from the Frenchman, and he ate it. That was Oscar Rothschild. When I left Buchenwald, he wrote me a little letter. When I'm in Holland, come and see me. Thank you for what you done, and all that. It's in a museum. That was Oscar Rotschild. What was the other one? Laying in Buchenwald one day, a man comes in, Italian, Luchi Olivi his name was. He comes in. I could see him walking. He had a long white kind of dress hanging, and a leather strap. And he comes to lay in the bunk opposite me. I can't speak Italian, so I don't how, but with gestures, we get to know one another. And he's got stomach trouble. And as the days going by, he can't have the soup. The soup doesn't stay. It gives over and over and over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then comes the last day, comes the last hour, and I watch him. Minute by minute, he's fighting death. Then it's quiet. I look in his eyes, his eyes go glassy. And then there's no-- nothing more. Then I think he's dead. And I call the Kapo. Kapo comes over. And then he's lift out of the bunk into a sheet. Two men get to hold of the corners of the sheet, carries him out. But before they do that, they put his number in big ink-- or whatever it is, ball pen-- on his chest. And he's written off. That's what they do. They're written off, finished. He's no more registered. And the doctor says to me, we all got to get out of-- there are orders from Germany that we've got to evacuate Buchenwald. I says, I can't. I can't walk hardly, and I don't feel like it. And he said, well I've got to go as well. I said, suppose you changed my name that I've died, and Luchi is Greenman. That was before he died. He said, we can't do that. Anyhow, the day comes. It's about 4:00. I'm ill. I'm still laying in bed. And whenever the Kapo comes in, he looks across the beds, and sees everybody under the blankets. And I'm also under the blankets. I think, if he only can accept that I'm not well, so I don't need to go out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, yeah. One afternoon-- one evening, I see SS put things underneath a barrack opposite me, a wooden barrack opposite me. Wooden barracks where I was-- and that wooden barrack had a staircase coming down on the side. So it had one on the-- and one floor on the top. Now I wondered what they were doing. And the next day-- and in between, you hear on the-- on the-- on the loud speakers, the Kapo see to it that the Jews and the homosexuals get to the square tomorrow morning. Every morning, that went on. They were taking the Jews, and all of those. Then I see this, the SS shooting their revolvers through the windows into the barrack. And all of a sudden, the doors open. And a lot of man come running out, down the staircase, lots of them. And they're taken to the woods. At the end, on both sides of the-- of the stairs, I can see four or five men dead. They were trampled upon. They're finished. I remember all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- where are we now, in time? What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Buchenwald, Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, no, no, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: April '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. April '45, I get out of bed. It's the 11th of April. I'm looking out of the window. It's quiet. I don't see no SS in the towers. Quiet, nobody about, no SS, only a little airplane circling the camp. And Rotschild, Oscar Rotschild laying there in his bunk, and he says, what's going on? And all of a sudden, I see a lot of men coming between the barracks, coming back from the woods where they're supposed to be shot. And then I realize the camp is free. That's about 4:00. And at 6:00, we hear through the loudspeakers, the Americans have liberated Buchenwald. Stay where you are. Keep a lookout for SS because for every strange face which you haven't seen before, the SS had to use your camp uniforms to escape. I hear this still.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were liberated at 6:00. Camp was liberated, I didn't go out straight away. A few days after, two days after, I went out. Shook hands with the American soldier in his Jeep. Talked to him, surprised-- Englishman from England, and all that. He says, would you like some biscuits? I said, yes, I wouldn't mind. So he gets out, gets to the boots at the back, opens the boots, oh, a box, cardboard with a-- or whatever it was-- with biscuits. And I don't know where all the hands came from. Everybody grabbed the biscuits, and I had none.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] Well then, the days go by. I goes to the commander and tell him who I am. And I want to get home. I want to get back to Holland. And he says, no hurry. We know who you are. We'll call you. Come back tomorrow morning. When I went back the next morning, there was a different commander. Those Americans from the day before were chasing the SS. And then, at last, the Americans opened the gates of Buchenwald. And the men went out, the prisoners went out, and they went to the villages and did the wrong things. Came back with chickens and radio sets, and probably did other things. And the population complained. And the American commander said, gates closed. No one is allowed out. You misbehaved. We don't want that to happen. And then I said to Sergeant somebody-- American soldier-- I said, I don't wan-- I haven't been out. I said, I've done nothing wrong. But I'd like to keep on talking with the soldiers. So it's in the-- it's in the museum. Leon Greenman has the right to come and enter through the gate at Buchenwald. Anyhow, then, then I got up a concert for them, for the boys. And I went home to my barrack with a whole lot of tin army rations, some food. And I walked around Buchenwald, near the ovens. I can show you some ashes in a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bundle of jackets were laying there, and straps, coats. And I said to an American soldiers standing there watching it, I said, they've taken everything away from me. Can I have a jacket? He said yes. I said, but you're not going to shoot? He said, no. You take, and take one for me as well. So I took it, a jacket-- a jacket I still got. I don't wear it. One good leather, black leather jackets. The Germans had the best goods. And a strap. It's in the museum now. And-- all right, then I made up my mind to-- yeah, the leather jacket had brass buttons. I wanted brass buttons. Who would want ordinary buttons on it? So when I came in that evening, there's a young Polish prisoner. He says, you, Englishmen. [POLISH]. You're late. So I said, yes. I says-- I said, look, I've got a jacket here. I want you to put some buttons on there. So he looks, he says, I got no buttons. You seize the buttons, and I'll do it. How much-- how much are you going to charge me? He says, five soups. I said, don't be silly-- five soups. Three soups. That's how we used to pay. So I got my soup. I used to give it to him because I had those rations, and I gave him rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out to find the buttons. I was on barrack. Heaps of clothes, heaps of clothes, all kinds. So I crawled over it, looked at the things, and then I found a coat with buttons. Maybe the buttons were too big, but I didn't care. Took half a dozen buttons off, and it's-- it's on the coat, on the jacket, souvenirs. Anyhow, I was wandering around near the ovens. There were partly burned bodies in there, the bones. And outside, in front of the ovens, there were four big containers. Like that high, to the rim with bones and ashes. And it was a shock for me, you know. And I looked, and I thought, they won't believe this. So I couldn't help-- I took a handful of ashes. I took them home. I got them in a box here, only a little bit. Every time I look at it, it was less and less. I'll show it to you in a minute. But they're from Buchenwald. Then comes a day, the 24th, 25th of April. I had enough. I want to go home. So I go home. And I'm walking on the road outside of Buchenwald, and I see Americans. And I want to fly to Holland. So I says, anybody here can take me to a airport, I want to fly. Nobody was flying. So I walked. I walk about the road there, and along comes a Jeep next to me, stops next to me where I'm walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, you're the man that wants to fly. I said, yes, are you? He said, no. He says, here's a lady from London wants to talk to you. And this lady was Anne Matthews from the She was a journalist. And she said to me, tell me something about the camp. I said, if you put my name and address in London in the paper so that my brothers in England can read it, I'll tell you. And I'll show you afterwards. I'll show it. So she did it. So my-- the neighbor in the street where my brother lives, she came in running to my brother one evening. Isn't this your brother? Have a look. And then they knew I was alive. So walking around the airport, I couldn't get no plane to go to Holland. There weren't no such a thing. So they took me by airplane to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Paris-- in the plane, I had to tell the-- there was one high soldier there in a uniform. And the others were just soldiers, only about half a dozen. And I had to tell them what happened and all that. So I told them. And getting out the airplane, we walked through the canteen in the airport, and I saw a tray with donuts. So I looked, and they said, go on. So I took a donut because I was hungry. So he said, put some in your pocket. So I put some in my pockets. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they took me in a Jeep to the British consul. I got to the British consul inside of-- in a room. And it was a big desk, broad desk. One side, the man was sitting there, and I had to sit in the chair on the other side. And this must have been about 6:00. And this man didn't utter a word to me, right through the evening. About half past 12:00, he said-- he looked up from his book, whatever he was writing or doing. He said, are you hungry? Oh, I said, you're talking. I said, yes I'm hungry. He said, we'll take you to a place where you'll have something to eat. And they took me to a place. It was a house where people coming back from camps could settle down for a moment. And I think half past 1:00, I was having fried potatoes and greens, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they gave me a room all to myself for the first time-- a bed to myself. I got up the next morning-- usu-- usually in the camp, you get up early. I got up early, washed, bath, and all that, showered. Then I went down to the room for breakfast, and there were a lot of people sitting there. And probably I was not looking normal. And they all looked at me, and I said, well, not ready for the outside world yet. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'll fall back into a corner, and out comes an old lady. To me, she says, I'm Louise White. I live in London near Chiswick somewhere. She lived in London. She says, can I pour your tea? I said yes. So we got in conversation. She says, I'm going to London. Day after tomorrow, I'm going to London. I said, well, if you go, go down there, there, that and that. It's my brother's. Tell him you met me. I want to know-- let him know that I'm alive. And she said, yes, I'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: After my breakfast, I went out, and I walked an hour and a half to the British consul. I knew no way. I had no money. I couldn't speak proper French. Only on the paper it was the British consul, somebody, I think. I found the consul. I had to talk to somebody in the consul. I wanted a passport. I want to get to Holland. I wanted to do a bit of fighting. He said, no, you can't go to Holland. Go home where you are. We know where you are. We'll call you when necessary. And I went back. And I couldn't walk anymore after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in France for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four hours-- four months, I think. Must have been about four months. So I showed-- and the old lady was a nurse. She had stayed in France looking after an old lady. And I said, look I can't walk. Perhaps you can put a bandage around my foot. I can't walk. And she had a look. She said no, I can't do nothing with that. She said the hospital is down the road here, not far from here. Go to the hospital. I said, I don't want to go to a hospital. She says, well, I can't help you. If you don't go, I'm not going to your brother's. Oh, [INAUDIBLE] I better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the hospital. I've got a name I remember-- [FRENCH] British-- Hertford British Hospital. I remember the man at the door was an old soldier out there saying, what do you want? I said, I want to see a doctor-- my foot. Who are you, and so on. So I saw Dr. Schwartz, Jewish doctor. I told him who I am, what I-- I said, I can't, my foot, I want a bandage around here. So he had a look at the foot, and a doctor knows, and he says, come in to the hospital. I says, no. He says, come in because I can't put a bandage around there. Something's got to be done to it. And I said, can I have a bath? He says, you can have six baths. Come on. Come in. So I came into hospital. I had a shower. I went into a bed. And I fell asleep. And he told me, also, before that-- he was telling me my parents went also to Auschwitz. So it went. Dark in the evening, about 10:00, maybe, the doctor comes in the ward and stops at every bed. And he comes to me, he says, you're not asleep? So I says-- I thought myself, I better tell him. I said, I'm hungry. He said, hungry? Oh, he says, you're the fellow from Buchenwald. So he gives instructions to the nurse, and up comes a tray and a big glass of milk and a couple of thick slices of bread. Now they were gone. Then I fell asleep. The next morning, he examined me. And then he wasn't satisfied with the foot, and he called in a French surgeon. And a couple of days later, he said, yeah, That toe's got to come off. I said, nothing off, nothing off. It's going to be-- no. He said, well, if you don't do it, your foot has got to come off. Of course, it's gangrene spreading. So I finally said, well, OK. So they operated on it. They strengthened me up. And the people behind the hospital saw us, come in to visit us. They brought along for me homemade cake and jams and all that, whatever they could spare. I had everything. I was so hungry. And then-- When-- I was going to say, when did you actually get back to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then after that, when I was strong enough, the doctor, well, you're all right. You leave hospital. I was taken in by French family until I could leave France. I had no money. I had nothing. The Quakers gave me some money. The Quakers gave me two pairs of trousers. One pair, I gave to the people where I was staying. One pair, I had on. The Swiss-- the British consul I went to, I said, I've got no money. They were prepared to loan me some money, and I think they loaned me 48 francs a month, something like that, which, a couple years after, they wanted back. Yeah. They want to stop me and my passport because I hadn't repaid them and all that. Well then I left France. I got my papers from France. Oh, they're so particular. Through Belgium, very slowly, the trains into Holland, and I stood in front of the house where I used to live on the 10th of October, 1945. I'd been away three years and two days. And I saw a young man on the streets. This was in evening. I said go there and there. It's Mr. Greenman.
